draft.introduction: Krishna explains the two paths: the yoga of knowledge for the contemplative, and the yoga of action for the active. Neither path works through inaction.

translation.text: The Blessed Lord said: In this world there is a twofold path: the yoga of knowledge for those inclined to contemplation, and the yoga of action for those inclined to action. Not by merely abstaining from action does one attain freedom, nor by mere renunciation does one attain perfection.
translation.original_text: śrī bhagavān uvāca | loke ’smin dvividhā niṣṭhā | jñāna-yogena sāṅkhyānām karma-yogena yoginām

translation.text: Therefore, without attachment, always perform the work that has to be done; for by performing action without attachment, one attains the Supreme. By action alone did Janaka and others attain perfection.
translation.original_text: tasmād asaktaḥ satataṃ kāryam karma samācara | asakto hy ācaran karma param āpnoti pūruṣaḥ

translator_notes.note: The distinction between jñāna-yoga and karma-yoga does not mean two separate religions. Krishna will eventually synthesize them: right action performed with right knowledge and offered to God constitutes the complete path.
